abstract: Agents equipped with search tools have emerged as effective solutions for knowledge-intensive tasks. While Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit strong reasoning capabilities, their high computational cost limits practical deployment for search agents. Consequently, recent work has focused on distilling agentic behaviors from LLMs into Small Language Models (SLMs). Through comprehensive evaluation on complex multi-hop reasoning tasks, we find that despite possessing less parametric knowledge, SLMs invoke search tools less frequently and are more prone to hallucinations. To address this issue, we propose \policy, a lightweight fine-tuning approach that explicitly trains SLMs to reliably retrieve and generate answers grounded in retrieved evidence. Compared to agent distillation from LLMs, our approach improves performance by 17.3 scores on Bamboogle and 15.3 scores on HotpotQA, achieving LLM-level results across benchmarks. Our further analysis reveals that adaptive search strategies in SLMs often degrade performance, highlighting the necessity of consistent search behavior for reliable reasoning.

citations.fullCitation: Xiaoxi Li, Guanting Dong, Jiajie Jin, Yuyao Zhang, Yujia Zhou, Yutao Zhu, Peitian Zhang, and Zhicheng Dou. 2025. Search-o1: Agentic search-enhanced large reasoning models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2501.05366.
citations.justification: This paper is foundational as it defines the task of agentic search that the main paper addresses. The authors explicitly state they are formalizing their task in a manner similar to this work, making it a direct methodological predecessor.
citations.title: Search-o1: Agentic search-enhanced large reasoning models

citations.fullCitation: Minki Kang, Jongwon Jeong, Seanie Lee, Jaewoong Cho, and Sung Ju Hwang. 2025. Distilling llm agent into small models with retrieval and code tools. Preprint, arXiv:2505.17612.
citations.justification: This work is cited as the standard approach for agent distillation, which the main paper critiques and builds upon. The proposed 'Always-Search Policy' is presented as a superior alternative to the methods described in this reference.
citations.title: Distilling llm agent into small models with retrieval and code tools

citations.fullCitation: Zhilin Yang, Peng Qi, Saizheng Zhang, Yoshua Bengio, William W. Cohen, Ruslan Salakhutdinov, and Christopher D. Manning. 2018. Hotpotqa: A dataset for diverse, explainable multi-hop question answering. Preprint, arXiv:1809.09600.
citations.justification: This paper introduces the HotpotQA dataset, which is the primary benchmark used for both training and evaluation throughout the study. The main paper's central claims and results are validated using this specific dataset.
citations.title: Hotpotqa: A dataset for diverse, explainable multi-hop question answering

citations.fullCitation: Rishabh Agarwal, Nino Vieillard, Yongchao Zhou, Piotr Stanczyk, Sabela Ramos Garea, Matthieu Geist, and Olivier Bachem. 2024. On-policy distillation of language models: Learning from self-generated mistakes. In The Twelfth International Conference on Learning Representations, ICLR 2024, Vienna, Austria, May 7-11, 2024. OpenReview.net.
citations.justification: This citation is directly relevant to the methodology, as the paper implements On-Policy Distillation (OPD) as one of the specific techniques to incorporate the proposed 'Always-Search Policy'. It is a key component of the experimental setup to demonstrate the effectiveness of their approach.
citations.title: On-policy distillation of language models: Learning from self-generated mistakes

intermediateReport:
The following report details the research paper "Search, Do not Guess: Teaching Small Language Models to Be Effective Search Agents," outlining its context, objectives, methodology, findings, and implications.

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by a team of authors affiliated with multiple institutions:
*   Yizhou Liu (New York University)
*   Qi Sun (New York University)
*   Yulin Chen (New York University)
*   Siyue Zhang (Nanyang Technological University)
*   Chen Zhao (New York University)

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

The development of language model-based search agents represents a significant advancement in addressing complex, knowledge-intensive tasks. These agents operate by iteratively formulating search queries, retrieving information, reasoning over the gathered evidence, and adapting subsequent actions. Large Language Models (LLMs), typically comprising 7 billion parameters or more, have demonstrated strong reasoning capabilities and underpin most existing effective search agents, such as Search-o1 (Li et al., 2025).

However, the computational cost and latency associated with deploying LLMs present practical limitations for real-world applications. This challenge has motivated research into Small Language Models (SLMs), defined as models with fewer than 4 billion parameters, which offer greater efficiency. A key area of investigation involves distilling the agentic behaviors observed in LLMs into these more compact SLMs. Previous approaches to agent distillation (e.g., Kang et al., 2025) have focused on transferring skills like tool use and reasoning.

This paper identifies a specific limitation in the existing landscape of SLM search agents: despite their smaller parametric knowledge base, SLMs tend to invoke external search tools less frequently and are more susceptible to generating incorrect information, a phenomenon termed "parametric hallucination." Standard distillation methods have yielded only marginal improvements in addressing these issues. The presented work positions itself within this context by proposing a novel distillation approach specifically designed to mitigate these identified shortcomings in SLMs, aiming to enable them to perform as reliably as LLMs in search-augmented tasks while retaining their efficiency advantages. The paper also explores the viability of adaptive search strategies for SLMs, a mechanism proposed in broader agent research to reduce computational overhead, and assesses its applicability to SLMs given their inherent limitations.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The central motivation for this research stems from the disparity between the effectiveness of Large Language Models (LLMs) as search agents and their prohibitive computational costs, which restrict their practical deployment in scenarios sensitive to latency or budget. While Small Language Models (SLMs) offer a more efficient alternative, their performance in knowledge-intensive, multi-hop reasoning tasks, particularly when augmented with search tools, has been suboptimal.

The authors identify two primary issues underlying the underperformance of SLMs in this context:
1.  **Parametric Hallucination:** SLMs frequently over-rely on their limited internal parametric knowledge, leading them to generate speculative or incorrect answers rather than seeking external verification. This tendency is counter-intuitive, as models with less inherent knowledge might be expected to utilize external tools more often.
2.  **Insufficient Search Tool Invocation:** Contrary to expectations, SLMs exhibit a lower frequency of invoking external search tools compared to LLMs. This "under-searching" further exacerbates the problem of hallucination and prevents SLMs from acquiring the necessary evidence for accurate reasoning.

Furthermore, the paper highlights that traditional agentic distillation methods, which involve fine-tuning SLMs on trajectories generated by LLM teachers, yield only marginal improvements. This is attributed to the fact that LLM-generated trajectories often implicitly depend on parametric knowledge that is unavailable to the smaller student models, making direct imitation insufficient.

Given these identified challenges, the key objectives of this research are:
*   To thoroughly evaluate the agentic search performance of vanilla and standard-distilled SLMs on complex multi-hop QA benchmarks to precisely characterize their failure modes.
*   To propose and implement "Always-Search Policy" (ASP), a lightweight fine-tuning approach that explicitly trains SLMs to consistently retrieve information using external search tools and to ground their answers in the retrieved evidence, thereby mitigating parametric hallucination and under-searching.
*   To compare the effectiveness of ASP-incorporated distillation methods against standard agent distillation techniques and LLM performance across various knowledge-intensive benchmarks.
*   To investigate whether SLMs can adaptively decide when to search based on their internal confidence, and to determine if such adaptive strategies are beneficial or detrimental for SLM-based search agents.
*   To demonstrate that SLMs trained with ASP can achieve performance comparable to LLMs while maintaining their inherent efficiency advantages.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The research employed a multi-faceted approach involving initial evaluation, a novel distillation policy, and comparative analysis across various benchmarks and model architectures.

**4.1 Task Formulation**
Agentic search was formalized as a multi-step decision-making process. Given a question (Q), the agent generates a thought (s_t), takes an action (a_t) from an action space (A), and observes the outcome (o_t). The action space primarily includes `Search` (formulating a query and invoking an external tool) and `Answer` (terminating reasoning and returning the answer). An LLM summarizer condensed retrieved documents into observations.

**4.2 Initial Evaluation of Vanilla and Distilled SLM Agents**
*   **Models:** The Qwen3 series of models, ranging from 0.6B to 32B parameters, was used. Qwen3-1.7B served as the primary SLM target, and Qwen3-32B as the teacher LLM. Llama-3.2 models were also included for broader validation.
*   **Benchmarks:** Multi-hop QA benchmarks such as HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, Bamboogle, and MuSiQue were used for structured reasoning. Agentic & Information Seeking QA benchmarks including BrowseComp-plus, Frames, and LongSeAL assessed generalization.
*   **Metrics:** String-F1 and Exact Match (EM) scores were reported.
*   **Search Infrastructure:** Qwen3-32B was utilized as the summarizer, and the e5-large-v2 retriever (Wang et al., 2022) with the fullwiki-20210620 corpus (wik, 2021) was employed for retrieval.
*   **Vanilla Performance Assessment:** Models of varying scales were evaluated to establish baseline performance and tool-calling frequencies.
*   **Standard Agentic Distillation:** Qwen3-1.7B was fine-tuned using Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on a subset of 18,000 successful trajectories generated by the Qwen3-32B teacher on the HotpotQA training set. Trajectories with String-F1 scores below 0.65 were filtered.
*   **Error Analysis:** Human annotation was performed on a sample of failed cases from the distilled 1.7B model to categorize primary causes of error (e.g., insufficient retrieval, hallucination, reasoning error). This analysis aimed to confirm the hypothesis of parametric hallucination and under-searching.

**4.3 Distillation with Always-Search Policy (ASP)**
The core of the proposed methodology is the Always-Search Policy (ASP), a distillation paradigm designed to explicitly enforce search behavior in SLMs.
*   **Concept:** ASP mandates that SLMs must search for and ground all essential information with external tools before generating an answer, rather than relying on internal parametric knowledge. This is conceptualized as training a "Knowledge-Free" agent.
*   **Fine-tuning Techniques Incorporating ASP:**
    *   **SFT with ASP (SFT-ASP):** Standard SFT was applied to trajectories, but with enhanced filtering. Only trajectories where the teacher model consistently used search tools to obtain information (verified by checking for tool calls and filtering out phrases like "I remember") were retained for training.
    *   **On-Policy Distillation (OPD with ASP):** Instead of explicit trajectory filtering, a system prompt was inserted at the beginning of each interaction, explicitly instructing the SLM to always use search tools. The teacher model's log-probability distribution was then used to guide the SLM's behavior towards searching.
    *   **Mixed Setting:** OPD was performed on top of an ASP-incorporated SFT, aiming for reinforced search behavior.
    *   **Rejection Fine-Tuning (RFT):** Applied as a final stage after distillation to further exploit the capacity of the distilled models by selectively reinforcing high-quality agentic behaviors.
*   **Prompts:** Specific system instructions were crafted for both general agentic search and the "Knowledge-Free" agent under ASP, emphasizing the mandatory use of search tools and strict output formats.

**4.4 Investigation of Adaptive Search for SLMs**
*   **Confidence Probe:** To assess SLMs' ability to self-answer without search, a confidence probe was developed. This involved training a three-layer Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) classifier on the fixed hidden representations from the last four layers of the frozen backbone SLM. The probe was trained to predict the model's confidence in answering a query based on its internal knowledge.
*   **Adaptive Search Simulation:** Adaptive search was simulated by allowing SLMs to self-answer queries where their internal confidence (as determined by the probe) exceeded a certain threshold (top-P%). The performance drop was then measured for different P values to determine the viability of adaptive search for SLMs.
*   **Calibration:** The probes were calibrated to ensure reliable confidence signals, with Estimated Calibration Error (ECE) and accuracy metrics reported.

**4.5 Noise Ablation and Reasoning Ability Assessment**
*   **Robustness to Noise:** Experiments were conducted where 10% of retrieval results were replaced with failed or misleading information to evaluate the ASP-trained models' ability to recover and maintain performance in noisy environments.
*   **General Reasoning:** The general reasoning abilities of vanilla and ASP-trained SLMs were evaluated on benchmarks like MMLU, GSM8K, and GPQA to understand if ASP training conferred broader reasoning improvements.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research yielded several key findings regarding the performance of Small Language Models (SLMs) as search agents and the effectiveness of the proposed Always-Search Policy (ASP).

**5.1 Underperformance of Vanilla and Standard-Distilled SLMs**
*   **Vanilla SLMs:** Initial evaluations demonstrated that vanilla SLMs (e.g., Qwen3-1.7B) performed significantly worse than their larger counterparts (e.g., Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-32B) on multi-hop QA benchmarks. They exhibited a lower frequency of external tool calls (e.g., 1.72 searches per question for Qwen3-1.7B versus 3.02 for Qwen3-32B) and were prone to "parametric hallucination," where they generated speculative answers instead of searching for information.
*   **Standard Agentic Distillation:** Naive distillation from a large teacher model (Qwen3-32B) to an SLM (Qwen3-1.7B) resulted in only marginal performance improvements. For instance, on Bamboogle, String-F1 improved from 50.6 (vanilla SLM) to 53.2. This distilled model still exhibited a substantial performance gap compared to the teacher (47.9% vs. 60.3% on HotpotQA) and continued to under-search (1.89 tool calls vs. 3.02 for the teacher). Error analysis confirmed that insufficient search and hallucination were the primary causes of failure, accounting for a significant portion of errors. Providing pre-retrieved evidence dramatically improved SLM accuracy (from 47.9% to 74.7%), indicating that the main bottleneck was retrieval, not reasoning.

**5.2 Effectiveness of Always-Search Policy (ASP)**
*   **Significant Performance Improvement:** The Always-Search Policy (ASP) substantially enhanced the agentic capabilities of SLMs. SFT-Qwen3-1.7B with ASP achieved a String-F1 score of 70.6 on Bamboogle, a 17.4-point increase over the standard-distilled SLM (53.2), and 57.6 on HotpotQA (a 9.7-point increase). This performance was comparable to, and in some cases surpassed, that of Qwen3-8B. The performance gap to the Qwen3-32B teacher model was reduced to approximately 2.5 F1 points on Bamboogle.
*   **Consistent Tool Calling:** ASP effectively mitigated the "under-searching" tendency of SLMs. The average search frequency increased significantly (e.g., to 2.47 for SFT-ASP and 2.84 for OPD-ASP), indicating a more active and consistent use of external tools. This active tool-calling behavior was identified as crucial for reducing hallucination and improving overall performance.
*   **Generalization to Out-of-Distribution Tasks:** Models trained with ASP on HotpotQA demonstrated good generalization to more challenging and out-of-distribution agentic search tasks, including BrowseComp-Plus, Frames, and LongSeAL, with notable performance gains compared to vanilla or standard-distilled SLMs.
*   **Robustness to Noisy Retrieval:** ASP-trained models exhibited greater robustness to noisy retrieval environments. When 10% of retrieval results were replaced with failed retrieval, ASP-trained models experienced a significantly smaller performance drop (e.g., 2.3 and 1.7 points for SFT-ASP and OPD-ASP respectively) compared to vanilla SLMs or standard-distilled models (12.1 points). This suggests improved ability to recover from or validate against imperfect retrieval.
*   **Improved Reasoning Ability:** Beyond agentic search, ASP training also led to an improvement in general reasoning abilities for SLMs, as evidenced by higher scores on benchmarks like MMLU, GSM8K, and GPQA compared to vanilla SLMs.

**5.3 Ineffectiveness of Adaptive Search for SLMs**
*   **SLMs Lack Reliable Internal Knowledge:** Confidence probing revealed that SLMs possessed less reliable internal knowledge compared to LLMs. While the Qwen3-32B teacher model had high confidence in answering over 40% of queries with its internal knowledge, SLMs' confidence distributions indicated a lack of reliable internal knowledge for key information.
*   **Performance Degradation with Adaptive Search:** Simulating adaptive search, where SLMs self-answered queries with high internal confidence, resulted in significant performance degradation for SLMs, even at low confidence thresholds (e.g., a 4.8-point drop for SFT-Qwen3-1.7B at P=5%). In contrast, LLMs could self-answer a larger percentage of queries (e.g., >10%) with minimal impact on accuracy. This finding highlights that for SLMs, attempting to adaptively decide when to search is detrimental, and a consistent "always-search" policy is superior.

**5.4 Efficiency Gains**
*   ASP-trained SLMs, despite making more search tool calls on average, still achieved end-to-end inference latency approximately three times lower than that of the Qwen3-32B LLM, while delivering comparable performance.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The findings presented in this research have several significant implications for the development and deployment of language model-based agents.

**6.1 Enhanced Practical Deployability of SLM Agents:**
The most immediate impact is enabling the practical deployment of effective search agents in environments with stringent resource constraints, such as limited computational power or latency requirements. By demonstrating that SLMs (e.g., 1.7B parameters) can achieve performance comparable to much larger LLMs (e.g., 32B parameters) on complex knowledge-intensive tasks, the research provides a pathway for more efficient and cost-effective AI solutions. The reported three-fold reduction in inference latency for ASP-trained SLMs compared to LLMs underscores this practical advantage.

**6.2 Addressing Fundamental Limitations of SLMs in Agentic Tasks:**
The work rigorously identifies and directly addresses two critical failure modes in SLMs acting as search agents: "parametric hallucination" and "under-searching." The Always-Search Policy offers a principled solution by explicitly re-orienting the SLM's behavior towards external evidence grounding, rather than relying on its limited internal knowledge. This addresses a core challenge in making smaller models reliable for factual tasks.

**6.3 Novel Paradigm for Agent Distillation:**
The research introduces a distinct distillation paradigm that deviates from conventional methods. Rather than merely imitating the teacher's reasoning trajectories, ASP enforces a "Knowledge-Free" agent mentality during training, compelling the student SLM to always retrieve information. This approach is particularly significant because it suggests that for SLMs, simply learning to mimic LLM behavior (which may implicitly rely on vast parametric knowledge) is insufficient. Instead, explicitly structuring the learning process to prioritize tool invocation can lead to superior performance. This challenges existing assumptions in agent distillation and opens new avenues for designing more effective training strategies.

**6.4 Refined Understanding of Adaptive Agent Behavior:**
The investigation into adaptive search strategies provides a crucial insight: while adaptive tool-use might be beneficial for powerful LLMs, it is detrimental for SLMs. The finding that SLMs exhibit significant performance degradation even when self-answering a small percentage of queries with "high confidence" indicates that their internal confidence is often miscalibrated or insufficient for reliable autonomous decision-making regarding search. This highlights the importance of consistent and enforced retrieval for smaller models, suggesting that a simpler, "always-search" policy is a more robust design principle for SLM-based agents.

**6.5 Foundation for Future Research:**
The identified limitations of the current work, such as the relatively simple instantiation of ASP and the need for more rigorous characterization of SLM agent upper bounds, point towards promising directions for future research. This includes integrating ASP into more advanced training frameworks, exploring its effectiveness across a broader range of LM architectures, and developing mechanisms to robustly handle noisy or misleading information in real-world search environments. The observed improvement in general reasoning abilities of ASP-trained models also warrants further investigation into the synergistic effects of agentic training.
overview:
## The Challenge of Small Language Models as Search Agents

Language models have evolved into sophisticated "agents" capable of interacting with external tools to solve complex, knowledge-intensive tasks. These agents typically follow a cycle of formulating search queries, retrieving documents, and reasoning over the findings. While Large Language Models (LLMs) like the 32B parameter versions of Qwen3 have demonstrated high proficiency in these tasks, their deployment is often hindered by high computational costs and significant inference latency. This has spurred interest in Small Language Models (SLMs)—models with fewer than 4B parameters—which offer the efficiency required for real-time applications.

However, a performance gap persists. Smaller models often struggle with "parametric hallucination," where they attempt to answer questions using their limited internal knowledge rather than seeking external verification. This research addresses this disparity by introducing the Always-Search Policy (ASP), a framework designed to teach SLMs to act as "knowledge-free" agents that prioritize external search over internal speculation.

![Performance comparison of various SLM distillation methods and the teacher model.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04651v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: Comparison of the Always-Search Policy (ASP) against vanilla SLMs, standard distillation, and adaptive search strategies. The "Adaptive Search Trap" illustrates the performance drop when SLMs attempt to rely on their own internal confidence.*

## Understanding the Failures of Vanilla SLMs

Current research identifies two primary reasons why SLMs underperform as search agents compared to their larger counterparts: under-searching and parametric hallucination.

1.  **Under-Searching:** Counter-intuitively, models with less internal knowledge do not necessarily search more. Evaluations show that a 1.7B parameter model may only invoke search tools an average of $1.72$ times per question, whereas a 32B model invokes them $3.02$ times. This lack of curiosity prevents the SLM from gathering the necessary evidence to answer multi-hop questions correctly.
2.  **Parametric Hallucination:** SLMs frequently rely on their internal parametric weights to "guess" an answer. Because their internal knowledge base is smaller and potentially outdated, these guesses are often incorrect.

Standard distillation methods, which involve training a student SLM to mimic the trajectories of a teacher LLM, have proven insufficient. The teacher's trajectories often rely on implicit reasoning derived from its vast parametric knowledge—knowledge that the student simply does not possess. Consequently, the student fails to replicate the teacher's success because it cannot bridge the gap between the evidence it retrieves and the reasoning required.

![Impact of model scale on accuracy and search frequency.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04651v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: Analysis showing that as model size increases, both accuracy and the average number of tool calls rise. SLMs under-utilize tools compared to larger models.*

## Formalizing Agentic Search

To improve SLM performance, the researchers formalize agentic search as a multi-step decision process. For a given question $Q$, the agent generates a sequence of thoughts, actions, and observations. A typical trajectory $\tau$ is represented as:

$$ \tau = (Q, s_1, a_1, o_1, s_2, a_2, o_2, \dots, s_n, a_n) $$

Where:
*   $Q$ is the initial user query.
*   $s_t$ is the model's internal reasoning or "thought" at time $t$.
*   $a_t$ is an action chosen from the action space $A$.
*   $o_t$ is the observation or feedback received from the environment (e.g., search results).

The action space is defined as:

$$ A = \{ \text{Search}, \text{Answer} \} $$

In this framework, the agent must decide at each step whether to continue searching for more information or to terminate the process and provide a final answer.

## The Always-Search Policy (ASP)

The core contribution of this work is the Always-Search Policy (ASP). Rather than allowing the model to decide when it "knows enough," ASP forces the SLM to ground every piece of information in external evidence. This effectively treats the SLM as a "knowledge-free" reasoning engine.

The researchers implemented ASP through several fine-tuning strategies:

### Supervised Fine-Tuning with ASP (SFT-ASP)
Standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) uses successful trajectories from a teacher model. SFT-ASP adds a strict filter: only trajectories where the teacher model consistently used search tools to obtain information are used for training. If the teacher generated an answer without a clear search step or used phrases like "I remember," that trajectory was discarded. This ensures the student only learns behaviors grounded in retrieval.

### On-Policy Distillation (OPD-ASP)
To further reinforce this behavior, the researchers used on-policy distillation. A system prompt explicitly instructs the SLM to always use search tools. During training, the student model's output distribution is aligned with the teacher's, but the process is biased toward the "Search" action.

### Rejection Fine-Tuning (RFT)
As a final refinement, Rejection Fine-Tuning is applied. The model generates multiple candidate trajectories, and only the highest-quality ones—those that successfully reach the correct answer through thorough searching—are used for a final round of fine-tuning.

## Experimental Results and Generalization

The effectiveness of ASP was tested across several multi-hop reasoning benchmarks, including HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, and Bamboogle. The results showed a substantial improvement in the agentic capabilities of SLMs.

For example, on the Bamboogle benchmark, a standard-distilled 1.7B model achieved an $F1$ score of 53.2. By applying ASP, this score jumped to 70.6, coming within 2.5 points of the 32B teacher model (73.1). More importantly, the search frequency of the 1.7B model increased to 2.84 calls per question, closely matching the behavior of the teacher.

Beyond performance on standard benchmarks, the ASP-trained models demonstrated:
*   **Robustness to Noise:** When 10% of retrieval results were intentionally corrupted or replaced with irrelevant information, ASP-trained models showed higher resilience, dropping only 1.7 to 2.3 points in accuracy compared to a 12.1-point drop for standard models.
*   **Zero-Shot Generalization:** Models trained on HotpotQA were able to generalize to more complex, out-of-distribution tasks like BrowseComp-Plus and LongSeAL without additional training.
*   **Efficiency:** Despite the increased number of search calls, the ASP-trained 1.7B model maintained an inference latency approximately $3 \times$ lower than the 32B model.

## The Adaptive Search Trap

A common approach in agent research is "adaptive search," where a model only invokes a search tool if its internal confidence falls below a certain threshold. While this strategy saves computational resources for LLMs, the researchers found it to be detrimental for SLMs.

By training a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) as a confidence probe on the frozen backbone of the SLM, the researchers observed that SLMs lack reliable internal knowledge. As shown in Figure 3, the confidence distribution for SLMs is heavily skewed toward low-confidence states compared to the 32B teacher.

![Confidence distribution of SLMs versus LLMs.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04651v1/x3.png)
*Figure 3: Histograms showing the internal confidence of various models. SLMs exhibit significantly lower confidence in their parametric knowledge, making them poor candidates for adaptive search.*

In simulations, even when the SLM was allowed to self-answer only $5\%$ of the time (where its internal confidence $P$ was highest), its performance dropped significantly. For the SFT-Qwen3-1.7B model, this led to a $4.8$ point decrease in accuracy. This "Adaptive Search Trap" suggests that for small models, a consistent policy of "search, do not guess" is always superior to trying to decide when to search.

## Conclusion and Future Outlook

The "Search, Do not Guess" framework provides a clear path for making Small Language Models as effective as their larger counterparts in knowledge-intensive tasks. By shifting the focus from parametric knowledge to active retrieval, the Always-Search Policy (ASP) mitigates the inherent limitations of SLMs—specifically hallucination and under-searching.

The significance of this work lies in its practical application. It demonstrates that with the right distillation strategy, high-performing search agents can be deployed on edge devices or in low-latency environments without the massive overhead of 30B+ parameter models. Future research may explore more complex versions of ASP that can handle even more diverse toolsets or integrate more sophisticated reasoning patterns into the "knowledge-free" paradigm.

keyInsights: The primary bottlenecks for Small Language Models (SLMs) in agentic search are parametric hallucination and insufficient search tool invocation, rather than reasoning ability once evidence is provided.
Explicitly enforcing an "Always-Search Policy" during distillation, rather than merely imitating teacher trajectories, is crucial for SLMs to effectively ground answers in external evidence.
Adaptive search strategies, where SLMs attempt to self-answer based on internal confidence, are detrimental to their performance due to unreliable internal knowledge, unlike for larger models.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) are computationally expensive for search agent tasks, hindering practical deployment due to high latency and cost.

Small Language Models (SLMs) struggle with knowledge-intensive tasks, exhibiting "parametric hallucination" by relying on limited internal knowledge and "under-searching" by failing to invoke external tools frequently.
Traditional agentic distillation methods for SLMs yield only marginal improvements, as LLM-generated trajectories implicitly depend on parametric knowledge unavailable to smaller models.
results: The "Always-Search Policy" (ASP) improved Small Language Model (SLM) performance, increasing String-F1 on Bamboogle by 17.4 points (from 53.2 to 70.6) and on HotpotQA by 9.7 points (from 47.9 to 57.6) for Qwen3-1.7B, achieving performance comparable to Qwen3-8B.

ASP-trained SLMs demonstrated an approximately three-fold reduction in end-to-end inference latency compared to a Qwen3-32B Large Language Model, while closing the performance gap to within roughly 2.5 F1 points on benchmarks like Bamboogle.

ASP effectively increased search tool invocation frequency (e.g., up to 2.84 searches per question for OPD-ASP) and enhanced robustness to noisy retrieval, resulting in smaller performance drops (2.3 vs. 12.1 points) than standard-distilled models.
solution: Introduced the "Always-Search Policy" (ASP), a distillation paradigm that explicitly trains Small Language Models (SLMs) to consistently invoke external search tools.
Implemented ASP through Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT-ASP) and On-Policy Distillation (OPD with ASP), filtering teacher trajectories to enforce evidence grounding and tool usage.
Designed specific system prompts to instruct SLMs to behave as "Knowledge-Free" agents, always searching for and grounding essential information externally.
summary.summary: Researchers from New York University and Nanyang Technological University introduce the "Always-Search Policy" (ASP), a distillation approach that explicitly trains small language models (SLMs) to consistently invoke external search tools and ground their answers in retrieved evidence. This enables SLMs to achieve performance comparable to large language models on complex knowledge-intensive QA tasks, while reducing inference latency by approximately three times, by effectively addressing parametric hallucination and insufficient search tool invocation.

title: Search, Do not Guess: Teaching Small Language Models to Be Effective Search Agents
